| Subject Section |

| Tribus: Semi-automated discovery of cell identities and phenotypes |
| from multiplexed imaging and proteomic data |
| |
| Ziqi Kang^1^, Angela Szabo^1^, Teodora Farago^1^, Fernando |
| Perez-Villatoro^1^, Inga-Maria Launonen^1^, Ella Anttila^1^, Kevin |
| Elias^2^, Julia Casado^1^, Peter K. Sorger^3^, Anniina |
| Färkkilä^1,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6^ |
| |
| 1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, |
| Finland |
| |

| Harvard Medical School, MA, USA |

| **Abstract** |

nki.fi]{.underline}](mailto:anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi)). |
| |
| Associate Editor: XXXXXXX |

| Received on XXXXX; revised on XXXXX; accepted on XXXXX |
| |
| **Abstract** |
| |
| **Motivation:** Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution has |
| been widely adapted to decipher the role of the tumor |
| microenvironment in cancer and other complex diseases. Accurate |
| cell-type phenotyping based on marker combinations is crucial but |
| challenging because of (i) fluorescence noise and batch effects, (ii) |
| segmentation artifacts, (iii) laborious annotation of the ground |
| truth, and (iv) difficulty in reproducing human-biased thresholding. |
| |
| **Results:** Here, we present Tribus, an interactive knowledge-based |
| classifier designed for multiplexed image and proteomic datasets that |
| avoids hard-set thresholds and manual labeling. We validated the |
| efficiency and accuracy of Tribus by benchmarking with two similar |
| methods on four public datasets with manual labels. Tribus can |
| recover fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts while |
| allowing the discovery of phenotypically new subtypes. We selected |
| two large in-house whole-slide image datasets to show Tribus yields |
| rapid and accurate cell-type calling in practical applications. |
| |
| **Availability:** Tribus is provided at |
| [[https://github.co |

image datasets to show Tribus yields |
| rapid and accurate cell-type calling in practical applications. |
| |
| **Availability:** Tribus is provided at |
| [[https://github.co |
| m/farkkilab/tribus]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus) |
| as an open-source package with a user-friendly Napari plug-in. |
+=======================================================================+
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

# Introduction 

Multiplexed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution, such as cyclic
immunofluorescence (CyCIF) (Lin et al., 2018), co-detection by indexing
(CODEX) (Black et al., 2021), and multiplexed ion beam imaging by time
of flight (MIBI-TOF) (Keren et al., 2019), have developed significant
advantages for understanding tissue architecture. Such techniques allow
researchers to measure dozens of proteins at single-cell resolution
while retaining the spatial information of origin in the tissue section.
These experimental workflows generate datasets that include multiple
features and provide novel insights into the diversity of cellular
phenotypes and tissue behaviors (Spitzer & Nolan, 2016).

Cell-type annotation has become one of the most essential tasks and a
bottleneck in gaining full potential from multiplexed imaging
technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed

become one of the most essential tasks and a
bottleneck in gaining full potential from multiplexed imaging
technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed
images are the unexpected combinations of markers, signal noise, and
reproducibility limits caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type
callers (Xie et al., 2021) to multiplexed imaging datasets because they
are not designed to overcome segmentation noise, which does not exist in
the scRNA-seq data. The order-of-magnitude difference in the number of
features is also an important problem. Several markers can dominate the
clustering process in multiplexed imaging techniques, and thousands of
genes have been provided to scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

Gating (Staats et al., 2019) is a classical cell-type annotation method
that requires manual identification of cell types based on the pattern
of features visualized using histograms and bivariate plots. However,
with the increasing size of the marker panel, manual gating becomes
time-consuming and sensitive to batch effects (Verschoor et al., 2015),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph (Levine et al., 2015), Leiden (Traag et al., 2019), and

ve to batch effects (Verschoor et al., 2015),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph (Levine et al., 2015), Leiden (Traag et al., 2019), and
DBscan (Ester et al., 1996) have been applied to perform automatic data
exploration (Liu et al., 2019). However, manual verification is still
required to assign meaningful cell types to the obtained clusters. To
perform a deeper profiling of cell types, it is usually necessary to
over-cluster and then merge the clusters, which is both computationally
and time-consuming. An alternative approach is to use supervised or
semi-supervised machine learning methods (Abdelaal et al., 2019), which
were developed to perform accurate auto cell-type classification.
However, such methods require pre-training on manually gated datasets
according to various experimental designs, thus requiring extra human
effort and lacking extendibility.

Here, we introduce the design of a novel cell-type caller named the
Tribus. Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations
across variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) (Kohonen, 1982) unsupervised neural network model with a unique
scoring function to assign cell types according to prior biological
knowledge.

widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) (Kohonen, 1982) unsupervised neural network model with a unique
scoring function to assign cell types according to prior biological
knowledge. An integrated Napari plugin allows for fast user interaction
and curation. We validated the accuracy of Tribus on four public
multiplexed imaging and non-spatial proteomic datasets generated by the
CODEX and mass cytometry methods. We show that Tribus outperforms two
similar knowledge-based cell-type identification approaches: ACDC (Lee
et al., 2017) and Astir (Geuenich et al., 2021), and demonstrate the
utility of Tribus in the analysis of two large in-house CyCIF datasets.
Overall, Tribus represents a new user-friendly framework for
semi-automated cell-type calling for multiplexed imaging and proteomics
techniques.

# Material and methods

## 2.1 The structure of Tribus {#the-structure-of-tribus .unnumbered}

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and prior knowledge-based logic
table are required as inputs. The logic table can be defined as a data
matrix Lci,mj containing values -1,0,1, where ci is the cell type ith
and mj is the marker jth. If a marker is present in a specific cell
type, the value in the logic table is assigned a value of 1. If a marker
is supposed to be absent in a specific cell type, then the value in the
logic table should be assigned as -1. A score of 0 was assigned for

e is assigned a value of 1. If a marker
is supposed to be absent in a specific cell type, then the value in the
logic table should be assigned as -1. A score of 0 was assigned for
neutral or unknown markers. Each cell type had at least one positive
marker in the logic table (Supplementary Table 1-3).

Tribus consists of two main parts. First, Tribus uses unsupervised
learning to find clusters from the input data, and then assigns each
cluster to a certain cell type based on the logic table. The cell type
assignment is performed hierarchically, which means that Tribus first
assigns lower-level cell types and then assigns higher-level cell types
to create more precise categories.
